title: Evaluate Expressions with Parentheses
prompt_md:
## Goal
Read two integers and print the results of three expressions involving them, demonstrating how parentheses change operator precedence.

## Input
- Line 1: integer A
- Line 2: integer B

## Output
Print exactly three lines:
- `no parens: <result>` — value of `A + B * 2`
- `parens add first: <result>` — value of `(A + B) * 2`
- `parens mul first: <result>` — value of `A + (B * 2)` (same as no parens)

## Requirements
1. Read A and B from stdin.
2. Print each result on its own line with the exact label and a colon-space before the value.
3. A and B fit in an `int`; results also fit in an `int`.

test_cases.label: Sample: a=3 b=4
test_cases.expected_stdout:
no parens: 11
parens add first: 14
parens mul first: 11

test_cases.label: Hidden: a=0 b=5
test_cases.expected_stdout:
no parens: 10
parens add first: 10
parens mul first: 10

test_cases.label: Hidden: a=10 b=3
test_cases.expected_stdout:
no parens: 16
parens add first: 26
parens mul first: 16

title: Fix Chained Subtraction
prompt_md:
## Goal
The program below is supposed to read three integers and print their left-to-right subtraction result (i.e., `(a - b) - c`). It contains one logic bug related to operator associativity — find and fix it.

## Input
- Line 1: integer A
- Line 2: integer B
- Line 3: integer C

## Output
Print exactly one line: `result: <value>` where value is `(A - B) - C`.

## Requirements
1. The fix must involve only how the expression is grouped — do not reorder the variables.
2. The corrected program must print the right answer for all inputs.
3. Edge case: A = B = C = 0 should print `result: 0`.
